popular_quote: To err is human; to forgive, divine.
author: Alexander Pope
snippet_in_source:
Good Nature and Good Sense must ever join;
To err is Human; to Forgive, Divine.
_curation_note: Closing couplet, Part II. Capitalization/italics per 1711 first edition convention.

popular_quote: A little learning is a dangerous thing.
author: Alexander Pope
discrepancy_note: Often misquoted as 'A little knowledge is a dangerous thing'; original is 'learning'.
snippet_in_source:
A little Learning is a dang'rous Thing;
Drink deep, or taste not the Pierian Spring:
There shallow Draughts intoxicate the Brain,
And drinking largely sobers us again.
_curation_note: Part II, opening of the famous quatrain. 'Dangerous' is contracted in original.

popular_quote: Hope springs eternal in the human breast.
author: Alexander Pope
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; popular shorthand 'Hope springs eternal' drops the rest.
snippet_in_source:
Hope springs eternal in the human breast:
Man never Is, but always To be blest:
The soul, uneasy and confin'd from home,
Rests and expatiates in a life to come.
_curation_note: Epistle I, lines 95-98.

popular_quote: Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold.
author: W.B. Yeats
_curation_note: Opening quatrain.

popular_quote: Beauty is truth, truth beauty — that is all ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.
author: John Keats
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; debated whether the urn or the poet speaks the line.
snippet_in_source:
Beauty is truth, truth beauty,—that is all
Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.
_curation_note: Closing couplet. Quotation marks and em-dash per Keats's 1820 ms.

popular_quote: If you can keep your head when all about you are losing theirs and blaming it on you.
author: Rudyard Kipling
snippet_in_source:
If you can keep your head when all about you
Are losing theirs and blaming it on you,
If you can trust yourself when all men doubt you,
But make allowance for their doubting too;
_curation_note: Opening quatrain. Indented even-numbered lines per Kipling's original.

popular_quote: Oh, East is East, and West is West, and never the twain shall meet.
author: Rudyard Kipling
poem_title: The Ballad of East and West
discrepancy_note: Quote stops one couplet too early — Kipling continues 'But there is neither East nor West, Border, nor Breed, nor Birth, When two strong men stand face to face...' inverting the usual reading.
snippet_in_source:
Oh, East is East, and West is West, and never the twain shall meet,
Till Earth and Sky stand presently at God's great Judgment Seat;
But there is neither East nor West, Border, nor Breed, nor Birth,
When two strong men stand face to face, though they come from the ends of the earth!
_curation_note: Opening quatrain. The popular quote stops at 'meet' — the ironic reversal in lines 3-4 is what makes the poem.

popular_quote: The mind is its own place, and in itself can make a Heaven of Hell, a Hell of Heaven.
author: John Milton
snippet_in_source:
The mind is its own place, and in itself
Can make a Heav'n of Hell, a Hell of Heav'n.
_curation_note: Book I, lines 254-255.

popular_quote: Better to reign in Hell, than serve in Heaven.
author: John Milton
snippet_in_source:
Here we may reign secure, and in my choice
To reign is worth ambition though in Hell:
Better to reign in Hell, than serve in Heav'n.
_curation_note: Book I, lines 261-263.

popular_quote: Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.
author: Wilfred Owen
discrepancy_note: Owen quotes Horace's line ('It is sweet and fitting to die for one's country') only to call it 'the old Lie'. Popular usage often inverts Owen's intent and uses it sincerely.
snippet_in_source:
My friend, you would not tell with such high zest
To children ardent for some desperate glory,
The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est
Pro patria mori.
_curation_note: Closing tercet. Owen's whole point is in 'The old Lie' — pulling out only the Latin tag inverts the meaning.

popular_quote: In Flanders fields the poppies blow between the crosses, row on row.
author: John McCrae
snippet_in_source:
In Flanders fields the poppies blow
Between the crosses, row on row,
That mark our place; and in the sky
The larks, still bravely singing, fly
Scarce heard amid the guns below.
_curation_note: Opening rondeau quintain. Indentation per McCrae's manuscript.

popular_quote: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Often cited as 'Methinks the lady doth protest too much' — original word order is 'The lady doth protest too much, methinks.'
snippet_in_source: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.
_curation_note: Gertrude, Act III Scene II. Standalone — the line is the artifact.

popular_quote: To gild refined gold, to paint the lily.
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Popular shorthand 'to gild the lily' fuses two distinct images from the original.
snippet_in_source:
To gild refined gold, to paint the lily,
To throw a perfume on the violet,
To smooth the ice, or add another hue
Unto the rainbow, or with taper-light
To seek the beauteous eye of heaven to garnish,
Is wasteful and ridiculous excess.
_curation_note: Salisbury, Act IV Scene II. Often misquoted as 'gild the lily'; original is two parallel actions.

popular_quote: If music be the food of love, play on.
author: William Shakespeare
_curation_note: Orsino, Act I Scene I, opening of the play.

popular_quote: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers.
author: William Shakespeare
_curation_note: Henry V, St Crispin's Day speech, Act IV Scene III.

popular_quote: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears.
author: William Shakespeare
_curation_note: Mark Antony, Act III Scene II.

popular_quote: The quality of mercy is not strained.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
The quality of mercy is not strain'd,
It droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven
Upon the place beneath: it is twice blest;
It blesseth him that gives and him that takes.
_curation_note: Portia, Act IV Scene I.

popular_quote: Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?
author: William Shakespeare
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; commonly misread as 'where' (location) rather than 'why' — the line laments his name, not his absence.
snippet_in_source:
O Romeo, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo?
Deny thy father and refuse thy name;
Or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love,
And I'll no longer be a Capulet.
_curation_note: Juliet, Act II Scene II. 'Wherefore' = 'why', not 'where'.

popular_quote: Double, double toil and trouble; fire burn and cauldron bubble.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
Double, double toil and trouble;
Fire burn, and cauldron bubble.
_curation_note: Witches' refrain, Act IV Scene I.

popular_quote: To be, or not to be, that is the question.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
To be, or not to be, that is the question:
Whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer
The slings and arrows of outrageous fortune,
Or to take arms against a sea of troubles,
And by opposing end them.
_curation_note: Hamlet, Act III Scene I.

popular_quote: Busy old fool, unruly Sun, why dost thou thus through windows and through curtains call on us?
author: John Donne
snippet_in_source:
Busy old fool, unruly Sun,
Why dost thou thus,
Through windows, and through curtains, call on us?
Must to thy motions lovers' seasons run?
_curation_note: Opening quatrain. The indented short line is part of Donne's metrical scheme.

popular_quote: I celebrate myself, and sing myself, and what I assume you shall assume.
author: Walt Whitman
snippet_in_source:
I celebrate myself, and sing myself,
And what I assume you shall assume,
For every atom belonging to me as good belongs to you.
_curation_note: Section 1, opening tercet.

popular_quote: Do I contradict myself? Very well then I contradict myself, (I am large, I contain multitudes.)
author: Walt Whitman
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; the parenthetical 'I contain multitudes' is part of the printed line.
snippet_in_source:
Do I contradict myself?
Very well then I contradict myself,
(I am large, I contain multitudes.)
_curation_note: Section 51.

popular_quote: O Captain! my Captain! our fearful trip is done.
author: Walt Whitman
snippet_in_source:
O Captain! my Captain! our fearful trip is done,
The ship has weather'd every rack, the prize we sought is won,
The port is near, the bells I hear, the people all exulting,
While follow eyes the steady keel, the vessel grim and daring;
_curation_note: Opening quatrain.

popular_quote: I sing the body electric.
author: Walt Whitman
snippet_in_source:
I sing the body electric,
The armies of those I love engirth me and I engirth them,
They will not let me off till I go with them, respond to them,
And discorrupt them, and charge them full with the charge of the soul.
_curation_note: Opening quatrain of section 1.

popular_quote: You may write me down in history with your bitter, twisted lies, you may trod me in the very dirt but still, like dust, I'll rise.
author: Maya Angelou
snippet_in_source:
You may write me down in history
With your bitter, twisted lies,
You may trod me in the very dirt
But still, like dust, I'll rise.
_curation_note: Opening quatrain.

popular_quote: I eat men like air.
author: Sylvia Plath
snippet_in_source:
Out of the ash
I rise with my red hair
And I eat men like air.
_curation_note: Closing tercet.

popular_quote: Quoth the Raven "Nevermore."
author: Edgar Allan Poe
snippet_in_source: Quoth the Raven Nevermore.
_curation_note: Refrain — the line stands alone as the artifact.

popular_quote: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary.
author: Edgar Allan Poe
snippet_in_source:
Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—
While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,
As of some one gently rapping, rapping at my chamber door.
_curation_note: Opening quatrain.

popular_quote: The moving finger writes; and, having writ, moves on.
author: Omar Khayyam (trans. Edward FitzGerald)
discrepancy_note: FitzGerald's translation has become more famous than the Persian original; Khayyam's quatrains do not have a fixed canonical form, and FitzGerald's loose paraphrase has near-canonical status in English.
snippet_in_source:
The Moving Finger writes; and, having writ,
Moves on: nor all thy Piety nor Wit
Shall lure it back to cancel half a Line,
Nor all thy Tears wash out a Word of it.
_curation_note: Quatrain LI (1st edition; later editions renumber). Capitalization per FitzGerald.

popular_quote: A jug of wine, a loaf of bread — and thou.
author: Omar Khayyam (trans. Edward FitzGerald)
discrepancy_note: FitzGerald's loose English version; the Persian original is structurally different and is largely a creative reworking.
snippet_in_source:
A Book of Verses underneath the Bough,
A Jug of Wine, a Loaf of Bread—and Thou
Beside me singing in the Wilderness—
Oh, Wilderness were Paradise enow!
_curation_note: Quatrain XII (1st edition).

popular_quote: Odi et amo. Quare id faciam, fortasse requiris. Nescio, sed fieri sentio et excrucior.
author: Catullus
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'I hate and I love'. The complete two-line poem; commonly quoted only as the opening 'Odi et amo'.
snippet_in_source:
Odi et amo. quare id faciam, fortasse requiris.
nescio, sed fieri sentio et excrucior.
_curation_note: Complete poem (one elegiac couplet). Standard Mynors/OCT text. EN: 'I hate and I love. You may ask why. I do not know, but I feel it happen and am crucified.'

popular_quote: Carpe diem, quam minimum credula postero.
author: Horace
discrepancy_note: Often shortened to 'Carpe diem' = 'seize the day'; the fuller phrase is 'pluck the day, trusting tomorrow as little as possible' — closer to a memento mori than the modern hustle slogan.
snippet_in_source:
...sapias, vina liques, et spatio brevi
spem longam reseces. dum loquimur, fugerit invida
aetas: carpe diem, quam minimum credula postero.
_curation_note: Closing tricolon. EN gloss: 'Be wise, strain the wine, and prune long hopes to short spans. Even as we speak, jealous time has fled: pluck the day, trusting tomorrow as little as you can.'

popular_quote: Exegi monumentum aere perennius.
author: Horace
discrepancy_note: Identical to original — 'I have raised a monument more lasting than bronze'.
snippet_in_source:
Exegi monumentum aere perennius
regalique situ pyramidum altius,
quod non imber edax, non Aquilo impotens
possit diruere aut innumerabilis
annorum series et fuga temporum.
_curation_note: Opening lines. EN gloss: 'I have raised a monument more lasting than bronze and loftier than the kings' pyramids, which no devouring rain, no raging north wind can destroy, nor the countless succession of years and the flight of ages.'

popular_quote: Out, out, brief candle! Life's but a walking shadow.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
Out, out, brief candle!
Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player
That struts and frets his hour upon the stage
And then is heard no more.
_curation_note: Macbeth, Act V Scene V — pairs with the 'Tomorrow' speech (next entry).

popular_quote: Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, creeps in this petty pace from day to day.
author: William Shakespeare
_curation_note: Macbeth, Act V Scene V.

popular_quote: All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players.
author: William Shakespeare
snippet_in_source:
All the world's a stage,
And all the men and women merely players;
They have their exits and their entrances,
And one man in his time plays many parts,
_curation_note: Jaques, Act II Scene VII.

popular_quote: Anyone lived in a pretty how town.
author: E.E. Cummings
poem_title: anyone lived in a pretty how town
snippet_in_source:
anyone lived in a pretty how town
(with up so floating many bells down)
spring summer autumn winter
he sang his didn't he danced his did.
_curation_note: Opening quatrain. Lowercase per Cummings.

popular_quote: On either side the river lie long fields of barley and of rye.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
_curation_note: Opening quintain.

popular_quote: I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree.
author: W.B. Yeats
snippet_in_source:
I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree,
And a small cabin build there, of clay and wattles made;
Nine bean-rows will I have there, a hive for the honey-bee,
And live alone in the bee-loud glade.
_curation_note: Opening quatrain.

popular_quote: I have eaten the plums that were in the icebox.
author: William Carlos Williams
discrepancy_note: Identical to original; the source of the long-running 'plums meme'.
snippet_in_source:
I have eaten
the plums
that were in
the icebox

and which
you were probably
saving
for breakfast

Forgive me
they were delicious
so sweet
and so cold
_curation_note: Complete poem (12 lines + 2 stanza breaks). Williams's signature minimalism.

popular_quote: Good fences make good neighbours.
author: Robert Frost
discrepancy_note: Often cited as Frost's own moral; in the poem the line is the neighbour's saying that the speaker doubts.
snippet_in_source:
I see him there
Bringing a stone grasped firmly by the top
In each hand, like an old-stone savage armed.
He moves in darkness as it seems to me,
Not of woods only and the shade of trees.
He will not go behind his father's saying,
And he likes having thought of it so well
He says again, 'Good fences make good neighbors.'
_curation_note: Closing — the famous line is the NEIGHBOR's, not Frost's. Frost spends the whole poem questioning it.

popular_quote: First they came for the socialists, and I did not speak out — because I was not a socialist.
author: Martin Niemöller
discrepancy_note: No single canonical version — Niemöller spoke this in many forms; the popular English text varies in which groups appear and in what order.
snippet_in_source:
First they came for the socialists, and I did not speak out—
because I was not a socialist.
Then they came for the trade unionists, and I did not speak out—
because I was not a trade unionist.
Then they came for the Jews, and I did not speak out—
because I was not a Jew.
Then they came for me—and there was no one left to speak for me.
_curation_note: USHMM canonical English version. Niemöller used many variants in lectures (including German originals); no single 'authoritative' text exists. This is the most-cited.
